teaching.title: C++ Inheritance & Virtual Methods
teaching.analogy: C++ inheritance is EXPLICIT about polymorphism — you must declare methods 'virtual' to enable runtime dispatch. Without virtual, the compile-time type decides which method runs. This is a fundamental difference from Java where all methods are virtual by default.
teaching.content: class Dog : public Animal — public inheritance. virtual method: runtime dispatch by actual type. override keyword: verifies overriding. Don't forget virtual destructor! Abstract: pure virtual: virtual void fn()=0;

examples.title: Virtual Methods
Pure Virtual & Abstract Classes
examples.explanation: =0 makes method pure virtual — class is abstract.

teaching.funFact: In C++, forgetting 'virtual' on the destructor causes memory leaks when deleting via base pointer! 'delete Animal*' won't call Dog's destructor. Java has no this problem — all methods are virtual and GC handles memory. 🔴

mcq.q: C++ method dispatch without 'virtual' keyword:
mcq.explanation: Static Dispatch: Without virtual, C++ uses the declared type at compile time. Animal *a=new Dog(); a->speak() calls Animal::speak NOT Dog::speak. Must add virtual!

mcq.q: Why do C++ base classes need virtual destructors?
options: delete Animal* won't call Dog destructor without virtual — memory leak!
mcq.explanation: Memory Leak: delete base_ptr without virtual ~Base() only calls base destructor — skips derived. Resource leak! Always declare virtual ~Base() in polymorphic base classes.

mcq.q: virtual void area()=0 makes the class:
options: Abstract — cannot be instantiated, subclasses must implement
mcq.explanation: Abstract: Pure virtual (=0) means subclasses MUST override. Class with any pure virtual is abstract — cannot be instantiated directly.

mcq.q: override keyword in C++ does what?
options: Verifies at compile time that method actually overrides a virtual parent method
mcq.explanation: Compile-time Safety: void speak() override — compiler errors if speak isn't a virtual method in the parent. Prevents silent shadowing from typos. Use always!

coding.title: Virtual declaration
coding.objective: Declare speak() as virtual in base class Animal:
coding.hint: virtual keyword before return type enables runtime dispatch.

coding.title: override keyword
coding.hint: override after param list verifies this overrides a virtual function.

coding.title: Pure virtual
coding.objective: Make area() pure virtual in Shape:
coding.hint: =0 makes it pure virtual — no body, must override in subclass.

options: go() is not virtual — static dispatch uses declared type (A). Add virtual: virtual void go()
debugging.answer: go() is not virtual — static dispatch uses declared type (A). Add virtual: virtual void go()
debugging.hint: Without virtual, pointer type (A) determines dispatch. Add virtual for runtime polymorphism.

prediction.explanation: virtual dispatch: actual type is B. B::s() returns 'B'.

teaching.title: C++ Templates
teaching.analogy: A template is a cookie cutter — define the shape once, stamp out cookies in any material (int, string, float). The compiler generates a specific version for each type you use. Zero runtime overhead — all done at compile time.
teaching.content: template<typename T> — function or class template. Compiler instantiates for each type used. Function templates: auto-deduced. Class templates: explicit: Stack<int>. Compile-time polymorphism vs runtime (virtual).

examples.title: Function & Class Templates

teaching.funFact: C++ templates are Turing-complete at compile time! You can compute Fibonacci at compile time using template metaprogramming. C++20 constexpr and concepts made this cleaner, but template metaprogramming existed since C++98! 🤯

mcq.q: template<typename T> T add(T a,T b). add(3,4) — T is deduced as:
mcq.explanation: int: Arguments 3 and 4 are int literals. Compiler deduces T=int automatically.

mcq.q: C++ templates vs Java generics — key difference:
options: Templates: compiler generates separate code per type (no erasure). Java: type erasure — one class at runtime
mcq.explanation: Code Generation: C++ templates generate SEPARATE machine code per type — maxOf<int> and maxOf<double> are different functions. Java erases types at runtime — one bytecode.

mcq.q: Box<int> and Box<string> in C++ are:
options: Two different classes generated by compiler
mcq.explanation: Different Classes: Compiler generates Box<int> and Box<string> as completely separate classes. Unlike Java where Box<Integer> and Box<String> are the same class at runtime.

mcq.q: template<typename T, typename U> void fn(T,U). Called with fn(1,'a'). T=?,U=?
mcq.explanation: T=int,U=char: Arguments 1 (int) and 'a' (char) deduced independently. Multiple type parameters work independently.

coding.title: Template syntax
coding.objective: Write template declaration for function minOf<T>:
coding.starterCode:
___<typename T>
T minOf(T a,T b){return a<b?a:b;}

coding.title: Template use
coding.starterCode:
Box<string> sb("hello");
// sb.get() has return type: ___
coding.hint: Box<string>: T=string. get() returns T = string.

options: Passes by value — a,b are copies. Fix: void swap(T& a,T& b) to pass by reference
debugging.answer: Passes by value — a,b are copies. Fix: void swap(T& a,T& b) to pass by reference
debugging.hint: T a,T b = copies. T& a,T& b = references. Need & to modify originals.

teaching.title: STL Algorithms
teaching.analogy: STL algorithms are a toolkit of 100+ operations that work on any container via iterators. Instead of writing your own sort, search, or transform, you call the battle-tested standard version. They're faster, safer, and more expressive than hand-written loops.
teaching.content: All from <algorithm>. Work with iterators: begin()/end(). Key algorithms: sort, find, find_if, count_if, any_of, all_of, none_of, transform, accumulate (from <numeric>), for_each, max_element, min_element, reverse.

examples.title: Core STL Algorithms
examples.explanation: Replace manual loops with expressive algorithms.

examples.title: transform, any_of, all_of

teaching.funFact: std::sort is one of the most optimized pieces of code ever written. Most implementations use introsort — a hybrid of quicksort, heapsort, and insertion sort that's always O(n log n) worst-case while being fast average-case! 🚀

mcq.q: sort(v.begin(),v.end()) sorts in:
mcq.explanation: Ascending: Default sort is ascending (operator<). For descending: sort(v.begin(),v.end(),greater<int>()) or lambda: sort(v.begin(),v.end(),[](int a,int b){return a>b;}).

mcq.q: find() returns v.end() when:
mcq.explanation: Not Found: find() returns the end iterator when element isn't found. Always check: if(it!=v.end()) before dereferencing!

mcq.q: accumulate(v.begin(),v.end(),0) computes:
mcq.explanation: Sum: accumulate(begin,end,init) sums all elements starting from init. For product: accumulate(begin,end,1,multiplies<int>()).

mcq.q: transform(v.begin(),v.end(),out.begin(),fn) does:
options: Applies fn to each element, stores results in out
mcq.explanation: Map Operation: transform applies fn to each element in [begin,end) and writes result to out. Like a functional 'map' operation.

coding.title: sort call
coding.objective: Sort vector v in ascending order:

coding.title: accumulate sum
coding.objective: Sum all elements in v starting from 0:

coding.title: any_of with lambda
coding.objective: Check if any element in v is negative:
coding.hint: x < 0 checks if element is negative.

options: find returns v.end() when not found — dereferencing end() is UB! Check: if(it!=v.end()) first.
debugging.answer: find returns v.end() when not found — dereferencing end() is UB! Check: if(it!=v.end()) first.
debugging.hint: Always check if(it!=v.end()) before dereferencing find() result.

teaching.title: ⚠️ FINAL BOSS — THE C++ GRANDMASTER
teaching.analogy: The C++ Grandmaster commands the full language: virtual dispatch, RAII, templates, STL, lambdas, references, const correctness, and zero-overhead abstractions. Prove you've mastered the most powerful general-purpose language.
teaching.content: Tests ALL C++ levels: virtual/override/pure virtual, virtual destructor, initializer list, const methods, RAII/unique_ptr, operator overloading, templates<typename T>, STL sort/find/accumulate, lambda captures, range-based for.

examples.title: Complete C++ Arsenal
examples.code:
✅ virtual method → runtime dispatch
✅ override → compile-time verification
✅ virtual~Base(){} → no leak on delete
✅ =0 pure virtual → abstract class
✅ template<typename T> — zero overhead
✅ RAII: ctor acquires, dtor releases
✅ unique_ptr — no manual delete
✅ sort/find/accumulate + lambdas
✅ const auto& — no copy in range-for
✅ int& ref — modify without pointer

teaching.funFact: C++ is the only major language that gives you full control over memory layout, zero-overhead abstractions, compile-time generics, RAII, and system-level access simultaneously. That's why it dominates game engines (Unreal), trading systems, browsers, and OS development! 🏆

mcq.q: Animal*a=new Dog(); a->speak() — speak() is virtual. Which runs?
mcq.explanation: Dog::speak: virtual enables runtime dispatch. Actual object (Dog) determines method.

mcq.q: Base class with virtual ~Base(){}. delete base_ptr calling derived destructor:
options: Both Derived then Base dtor (correct order)
mcq.explanation: Both in order: virtual destructor ensures derived dtor runs first, then base. Without virtual: only base dtor runs — resource leak!

mcq.q: template<typename T> T sq(T x){return x*x;} sq(4) returns:
mcq.explanation: 16: T deduced as int. sq(4)=4*4=16.

mcq.q: sort(v.begin(),v.end(),[](int a,int b){return a>b;}) sorts:
mcq.explanation: Descending: Lambda returning a>b means 'a comes before b if a>b' — descending order.

mcq.q: unique_ptr<T> provides RAII meaning:
options: Auto-deletes managed object on scope exit
mcq.explanation: Auto-delete: unique_ptr's destructor calls delete. Scope exit → destruction → no leak. No manual delete needed.

mcq.q: class A{virtual void f()=0;}. Can you do: A a;?
options: No — pure virtual makes A abstract, cannot instantiate
mcq.explanation: No: Abstract class (has pure virtual) cannot be instantiated. new A() is a compile error.

mcq.q: for(const auto& x:v) vs for(auto x:v) — performance difference?
options: const auto& avoids copying each element — important for large objects like strings
mcq.explanation: No Copy: auto x copies each element. const auto& binds a reference — zero copy. For large objects (string, vector), const auto& is significantly faster.

mcq.q: C++ 'override' keyword on a method that doesn't override anything:
options: Compile error — override verifies the function actually overrides a virtual
mcq.explanation: Compile Error: override tells the compiler 'this MUST override a virtual parent method.' If it doesn't (typo, signature mismatch), compile error. Use always!

coding.title: Final: Template maxOf
coding.objective: template<typename T> T maxOf(T a,T b). maxOf(8,3)=?

coding.title: Final: STL sum

options: show() is not virtual — compile-time dispatch uses Base type. Add virtual: virtual void show(). Also add virtual ~Base(){}
debugging.answer: show() is not virtual — compile-time dispatch uses Base type. Add virtual: virtual void show(). Also add virtual ~Base(){}
debugging.hint: C++ requires explicit 'virtual' for runtime polymorphism. Without it, pointer type (Base) determines dispatch.
